segments.title: British Naval Aviation as Fleet Air Arm reference
segments.summary: A book-review episode uses Ray Sturtivant's British Naval Aviation to frame early-war Fleet Air Arm carrier operations and a Graf Spee counterfactual.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing value as a naval aviation reference review. Dr. Clarke introduces British Naval Aviation as a picture-and-fact-rich Sturtivant work, reads through early-war Fleet Air Arm carrier examples, cautions against using fleet carriers for anti-submarine patrols, and then uses Ark Royal's search for Graf Spee to explore how a Skua strike might have changed Fleet Air Arm visibility.

evidence.note: The opening introduces the book as Ray Sturtivant's British Naval Aviation 1917 to 1990 and describes it as a photo- and fact-heavy reference with many chapters.
The early-war carrier section lists Ark Royal, Courageous, Furious, Argus, Hermes, Glorious, Eagle, and Albatross, then warns that fleet carriers should not be used for anti-submarine patrols.
The Force K and Graf Spee section explains Ark Royal's South Atlantic search and asks how history might differ if her aircraft had located or attacked Graf Spee.
The counterfactual section links a possible Skua role against Graf Spee to Fleet Air Arm public visibility, Taranto, Swordfish reputation, and aircraft-development implications.
